Keywords

Key Points:

Health disparities exist among socioeconomic status, race, gender, and geographic location

in interstitial lung disease (ILD) and sarcoidosis.

In ILD and sarcoidosis, access to care, stress, and environmental exposures are potential

determinants of health disparities, but research is needed to identify additional modifiable

determinants.

Avenues to achieve health equity in ILD and sarcoidosis include health policy, research, and

expanding collaborative clinical care models.

pages.text:
Interstitial lung disease (ILD), a clinically recognized group of diseases resulting in pulmonary

fibrosis, affects up to 200 individuals per 100,000 in the United States. Sarcoidosis has a wide

range of clinical manifestations including pulmonary fibrosis. Health disparities are prevalent in

both ILD and sarcoidosis around socioeconomic status, race, gender, and geographic location.

This review outlines the known health disparities, discusses possible determinants of disparities,

and outlines a path to achieve equity in ILD and sarcoidosis.










Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is a clinically recognized group of diseases that all result in

scarring of the lung interstitium, also termed pulmonary fibrosis.1, 2 Causes of ILD vary from

known etiologies, such as connective-tissue disease (CTD-ILD) and hypersensitivity

pneumonitis (HP), to unknown etiologies such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and

sarcoidosis. When individually considered each ILD is a rare disease,³ however, as a group the

prevalence of ILD approaches 200 individuals per 100,000 in the United States (US) and has

increased by 19.1% over the last decade, in step with an aging population.4, 5 Sarcoidosis, while

considered an ILD, has a range of pulmonary manifestations including airway obstruction6, 7 with

a prevalence between 126-160 per 100,000 individuals⁸ and as high as 519 per 100,000 among

Black females in the US.⁹ Death and disability attributable to ILD is on the rise relative to other

diseases,⁴ suggesting that resource allocation may be inadequate.

# Interstitial Lung Disease and Health Disparities

Interstitial lung disease (ILD), a clinically recognized group of diseases resulting in pulmonary fibrosis, affects up to 200 individuals per 100,000 in the United States. Sarcoidosis has a wide range of clinical manifestations including pulmonary fibrosis. Health disparities are prevalent in both ILD and sarcoidosis around socioeconomic status, race, gender, and geographic location. This review outlines the known health disparities, discusses possible determinants of disparities, and outlines a path to achieve equity in ILD and sarcoidosis.

# Overview of Interstitial Lung Disease

Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is a clinically recognized group of diseases that all result in scarring of the lung interstitium, also termed pulmonary fibrosis.1, 2 Causes of ILD vary from known etiologies, such as connective-tissue disease (CTD-ILD) and hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP), to unknown etiologies such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and sarcoidosis. When individually considered each ILD is a rare disease,3 however, as a group the prevalence of ILD approaches 200 individuals per 100,000 in the United States (US) and has increased by 19.1% over the last decade, in step with an aging population.4, 5 Sarcoidosis, while considered an ILD, has a range of pulmonary manifestations including airway obstruction6, 7 with a prevalence between 126-160 per 100,000 individuals8 and as high as 519 per 100,000 among Black females in the US.9 Death and disability attributable to ILD is on the rise relative to other diseases,4 suggesting that resource allocation may be inadequate.

pages.text:
Healthcare spending on ILD per patient is higher than the per patient cost of asthma and COPD

and is increasing over time.¹⁰ Diagnostic complexity of ILD and sarcoidosis leads to financial

burden and patient stress.¹¹ Delays in diagnosis¹¹ and initial misdiagnoses add to unmeasured

antecedent healthcare costs. Sarcoidosis often affects individuals among prime working years,

resulting in missing work, loss of earnings¹² and disability¹³ for some, which worsens the

financial impact of the disease.



While there is variation in symptoms, quality of life, and disease course of ILD and sarcoidosis,

one common thread is that health disparities are prevalent, and the burden of these diseases on

patients and healthcare systems is significant. We review health disparities present in ILD and

sarcoidosis by socioeconomic status, race, gender, and geographic location, discuss possible

determinants of disparities, and outline a path to achieve equity.



Socioeconomic Disparities



The diagnosis of ILD or sarcoidosis associates with significant cost for all, and especially

burdens low-income individuals.14, 15 Careful and consistent pharmacotherapy is paramount to

limiting fibrosis.15, 16 However, treatment imposes a significant financial millstone that has

increased over time.11, 15, 17 Individuals with low income or individuals in low-income zip codes

report barriers to obtaining medicines¹⁸ and difficulty accessing medical care.¹⁹ These barriers

translate to poor outcomes. Individuals with IPF and low income have worse progression free

survival²⁰ and experience death more often than lung transplant, regardless of disease severity

or other transplant eligibility factors (Figure 1).²¹ Individuals with sarcoidosis and low income

have worse lung function,¹⁸ radiographic stage,²² health-related quality of life (HRQoL)¹⁴ and

higher healthcare utilization as compared to those with sarcoidosis and higher income.⁸ In

addition to income, insurance differences associate with worse outcomes. Patients with IPF who

are un-insured or publicly insured are less likely to receive lung transplantation or inpatient

rehabilitation.²³ Patients with sarcoidosis who are un-insured or publicly insured have lower lung

function,¹⁸ more disease progression,²⁴ and worse dyspnea,²² perhaps secondary to barriers to

Racial Disparities



In the US, significant racial and ethnic disparities remain throughout healthcare²⁵ and prevail

among patients with ILD and sarcoidosis. Black individuals with ILD and sarcoidosis have more

advanced disease at time of diagnosis²⁶ and lower lung function7, 27 compared to non-Hispanic

White individuals. Black individuals with sarcoidosis have higher rates of hospitalization,²⁸

higher mortality,29, 30 and more organ involvement²⁶ compared to non-Hispanic White individuals

with sarcoidosis (Figure 2). Black individuals with sarcoidosis are prescribed higher doses of

corticosteroids³¹ compared to non-Hispanic White individuals, however, are less likely to report

medication adherence.³² Though less frequently diagnosed with IPF, Black individuals who

receive this diagnosis die at younger ages relative to White patients with IPF.³³ Multiple

determinants interact to produce racial disparities including but not limited to socioeconomics,

institutional racism, differential access to medical care, increased stress, and differential

environmental exposures.34, 35



Sex and Gender Disparities



Sex and gender modify chronic disease including ILD and sarcoidosis via biological,

environmental and social/behavioral interactions.³⁶ There is a complex relationship between sex

hormones and lung disease37 and their role is understudied in ILD³⁸ and sarcoidosis.³⁹ Gender

# Racial Disparities

In the US, significant racial and ethnic disparities remain throughout healthcare25 and prevail among patients with ILD and sarcoidosis. Black individuals with ILD and sarcoidosis have more advanced disease at time of diagnosis26 and lower lung function7, 27 compared to non-Hispanic White individuals. Black individuals with sarcoidosis have higher rates of hospitalization,28 higher mortality,29, 30 and more organ involvement26 compared to non-Hispanic White individuals with sarcoidosis (Figure 2). Black individuals with sarcoidosis are prescribed higher doses of corticosteroids31 compared to non-Hispanic White individuals, however, are less likely to report medication adherence.32 Though less frequently diagnosed with IPF, Black individuals who receive this diagnosis die at younger ages relative to White patients with IPF.33 Multiple determinants interact to produce racial disparities including but not limited to socioeconomics, institutional racism, differential access to medical care, increased stress, and differential environmental exposures.34, 35

# Sex and Gender Disparities

Sex and gender modify chronic disease including ILD and sarcoidosis via biological, environmental and social/behavioral interactions.36 There is a complex relationship between sex hormones and lung disease37 and their role is understudied in ILD38 and sarcoidosis.39

but not limited to traditionally gendered occupations, military service, hobbies, or tasks that may

lead to differential environmental exposures, and tobacco use.⁴⁰⁻⁴² Patients with IPF are

approximately twice as likely to be male, and male sex is a risk factor in the IPF Gender-Age-

Physiology calculation for predicting future lung function decline⁴³ and mortality risk at 1

year.⁴⁴ CTD-ILD conversely exhibits higher prevalence of females affected and higher female

mortality.⁴⁵ Although more prevalent among females, CTD-ILD demonstrates a more

progressive course in males, including higher frequency of usual interstitial pneumonia.38, 46 In

sarcoidosis, males tend to present with more advanced radiographic disease²⁶, however

females more commonly demonstrate worse outcomes by other measures, including lower lung

function,¹⁸ higher rates of hospitalization,²⁸ higher disability utilization following diagnosis,¹²

worse HRQoL,⁴⁷ and more depression¹⁹ and anxiety.⁴⁸ Interestingly, males with IPF and

sarcoidosis are more likely to be prescribed disease modifying agents than females.15, 49



Geographic Disparities



The prevalence and outcomes of patients with ILD and sarcoidosis vary by location.⁵⁰⁻⁵² US

regional sarcoidosis mortality also intersects with race, demonstrating the highest age-adjusted

death rates among Black females in the Midwest, and Black males in the Northeast. 53

Neighborhood-level disadvantage impacts the incidence of sarcoidosis,⁵⁴ and associates with

higher mortality and lower odds of lung transplant for IPF.⁵⁵ Residents of rural areas with IPF

present to ILD specialty care with higher oxygen utilization and lower pulmonary function

States.⁵⁸ Interestingly, once listed for transplant, waitlist outcomes are better among patients

who travel more than 360 miles to the center,⁵⁹ suggesting some favorable factors in extremely

long-distance patients who have the resources to navigate the system.



Determinants of Disparities



While disparities are apparent, driving factors are incompletely elucidated. Access to care,

stress, and environmental exposures are likely determinants of health disparities in both ILD

and sarcoidosis. More investigation is needed to identify additional determinants.



Access to health care in the US has improved over the last decade, however gaps remain.⁶⁰

Limited access to pulmonary specialists associates with worse respiratory health outcomes.⁶¹ In

IPF, delay in access to a specialty center associates with increased mortality.⁶² Individuals with

sarcoidosis report access challenges,¹¹ however, more research is needed to understand the

impact of limited access to care on sarcoidosis outcomes.



Patients with ILD and sarcoidosis report high stress levels.63, 64 Low socioeconomic status,⁶⁵

racial discrimination,⁶⁶ gender discrimination,⁶⁷ and neighborhood factors⁶⁸ all associate with

exacerbated stress. Higher stress levels associate with worse health outcomes including higher

mortality,⁶⁹ higher disease burden,⁷⁰ and worse HRQoL.⁷¹ Chronic stress upregulates pro-

inflammatory gene transcription, 72 and links to glucocorticoid receptor resistance.⁷³ This results

in increased pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-alpha which is thought to be part of the

pathobiology of both ILD⁷⁴ and sarcoidosis.⁷⁵ More work is needed to understand the impact

and biology of psychosocial and environmental stress on health outcomes and disparities in

both ILD and sarcoidosis.

# Determinants of Disparities

While disparities are apparent, driving factors are incompletely elucidated. Access to care, stress, and environmental exposures are likely determinants of health disparities in both ILD and sarcoidosis. More investigation is needed to identify additional determinants.

Access to health care in the US has improved over the last decade, however gaps remain.60 Limited access to pulmonary specialists associates with worse respiratory health outcomes.61 In IPF, delay in access to a specialty center associates with increased mortality.62 Individuals with sarcoidosis report access challenges,11 however, more research is needed to understand the impact of limited access to care on sarcoidosis outcomes.

Patients with ILD and sarcoidosis report high stress levels.63, 64 Low socioeconomic status,65 racial discrimination,66 gender discrimination,67 and neighborhood factors68 all associate with exacerbated stress. Higher stress levels associate with worse health outcomes including higher mortality,69 higher disease burden,70 and worse HRQoL.71 Chronic stress upregulates pro-inflammatory gene transcription,72 and links to glucocorticoid receptor resistance.73 This results in increased pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-alpha which is thought to be part of the pathobiology of both ILD74 and sarcoidosis.75 More work is needed to understand the impact and biology of psychosocial and environmental stress on health outcomes and disparities in both ILD and sarcoidosis.

pages.text:
Socioeconomic and racial disparities persist in exposure to environmental toxins.⁷⁶

Disadvantaged neighborhoods suffer higher exposure to air pollution from industry, tobacco

smoke, and automotive traffic, particularly in more densely populated areas.⁷⁷ Occupational

exposures,²⁰ air pollution exposure,²⁰ and inhalation exposures⁷⁸ are frequently reported by

patients with ILD and associate with lower lung function.⁷⁹ The role of tobacco smoke exposure

in ILD cannot be understated. A study of idiopathic interstitial pneumonias found the odds ratio

for ever smoking cigarettes to be 3.6 (1.3–9.8) in those who developed ILD,⁸⁰ suggesting the

importance of tobacco exposure to disease onset. Poor air quality from motor vehicle exhaust,

industrial, biomass, and indoor pollution has been associated with the production of reactive

oxygen species, decreased telomere length, and release of inflammatory cytokines leading to

complex interactions with patients’ genetic predispositions and lung damage.⁸¹



Environmental factors such as occupation, the household environment, and lifestyle associate

with the risk of developing sarcoidosis and respiratory symptom severity.⁸² Interestingly, one

study of Black American siblings found that indoor exposure to high humidity, water damage, or

musty odors was associated with a higher odds of developing sarcoidosis.⁸³ Few studies detail

the impact of environmental exposures on sarcoidosis progression.⁴² Increased respiratory

symptom severity has been associated with increased levels of air pollutants among individuals

with fibrotic pulmonary sarcoidosis.⁸⁴



Avenues to Address Disparities



In ILD, there have been few interventions to address disparities.⁸⁵ We are aware of no

interventions conducted to address determinants of health disparities in sarcoidosis. However,

there is a body of intervention research available to address disparities in other disease

outcomes which may be leveraged and tested. Avenues to address the immense health

pages.text:
disparities in ILD and sarcoidosis include: 1. Health policy that promotes environmental justice

and institutional and structural changes to address racism, 2. Research innovation and

inclusion, and 3. Collaborative clinical care and patient-centered programs (Figure 3).

Policy level interventions to improve health disparities in ILD and sarcoidosis are an avenue to

address disparities. Environmental policies that promote environmental justice are essential

given the poor health outcomes in patients with ILD and sarcoidosis associated with

environmental exposures. Confronting policies in the US that contribute to institutional and

structural racism is also imperative to addressing health equity for ILD and sarcoidosis.

Importantly, there remain challenges to implementation even after policy change. Despite the

1993 NIH requirement for inclusion and reporting of women and minorities in NIH-funded clinical

trials, there has not been a significant increase in clinical trial reporting results by sex, race or

ethnicity.⁸⁶ This suggests that while policy changes are important, successful implementation is

necessary to impact equity.

We recognize the need to align the gender, race/ethnicity, and socioeconomic characteristics of

clinical research participants with the population the research serves, and to improve diversity in

care providers and investigators as well. Research statements by the American Thoracic

Society⁸⁷ and National Institutes of Health⁸⁸ offer evidence-based solutions to improving

recruitment and retention of participants from populations that remain underrepresented in

research. Efforts to build a diverse pipeline of investigators and clinicians are underway.⁸⁹

In addition to improving representation in clinical and translational research, methods of

research including variables of interest must be carefully scrutinized and results interpreted with

care. Self-identified race may correlate with geographic ancestry contributing to genomic

variation,⁹⁰ but is primarily an intricate social construct. As a bundle of complex interacting

relationships race warrants an equally complex analysis.⁹¹ Advanced statistical support, which

# Disparities in ILD and Sarcoidosis

Disparities in ILD and sarcoidosis include:

1. Health policy that promotes environmental justice and institutional and structural changes to address racism
2. Research innovation and inclusion
3. Collaborative clinical care and patient-centered programs (Figure 3)

Policy level interventions to improve health disparities in ILD and sarcoidosis are an avenue to address disparities. Environmental policies that promote environmental justice are essential given the poor health outcomes in patients with ILD and sarcoidosis associated with environmental exposures. Confronting policies in the US that contribute to institutional and structural racism is also imperative to addressing health equity for ILD and sarcoidosis.

Importantly, there remain challenges to implementation even after policy change. Despite the 1993 NIH requirement for inclusion and reporting of women and minorities in NIH-funded clinical trials, there has not been a significant increase in clinical trial reporting results by sex, race or ethnicity.⁸⁶ This suggests that while policy changes are important, successful implementation is necessary to impact equity.

We recognize the need to align the gender, race/ethnicity, and socioeconomic characteristics of clinical research participants with the population the research serves, and to improve diversity in care providers and investigators as well. Research statements by the American Thoracic Society⁸⁷ and National Institutes of Health⁸⁸ offer evidence-based solutions to improving recruitment and retention of participants from populations that remain underrepresented in research. Efforts to build a diverse pipeline of investigators and clinicians are underway.⁸⁹

In addition to improving representation in clinical and translational research, methods of research including variables of interest must be carefully scrutinized and results interpreted with care. Self-identified race may correlate with geographic ancestry contributing to genomic variation,⁹⁰ but is primarily an intricate social construct. As a bundle of complex interacting relationships, race warrants an equally complex analysis.⁹¹ Advanced statistical support, which

stems from greater investment in research infrastructure and methods, is necessary to

appropriately dissect the package of characteristics captured by racial identification.⁹² Gender

also warrants special attention as it encompasses the social constructs of masculinity and

femininity and thus how patients experience the world and their interaction with medicine and

research³⁶. Gender should be considered apart from biological sex and also modeled as a

complex variable. Fully understanding the biologic and social constructs of race, ethnicity, sex

and gender in populations with ILD and sarcoidosis will allow for the development of actionable

and clear interventions for improving disparities.

Research innovation in interventions utilizing home-based monitoring and telemedicine is one

bright path forward. Global practice patterns of expert ILD clinicians surveyed in early 2021

found that 39% used some form of home monitoring. Those that utilized home monitoring rated

their delivery of care as good as or even more effective than with face-to-face visits.⁹³

Comprehensive home monitoring added to usual care in newly treated patients with IPF does

not improve overall HRQoL but significantly improves psychological well-being.⁹⁴ An area of

particular enthusiasm with clinical and research design importance is home spirometry, where

studies demonstrate good adherence, feasibility and reliability of home spirometry for individuals

with ILD⁹⁴⁻⁹⁶ and/or sarcoidosis.⁹⁷ Importantly in rare diseases such as ILD and sarcoidosis,

home spirometry or other remotely and repetitively collected data can enhance precision of and

power to detect effect, and thus diminish the patient sample size needed to detect a significant

difference in measured outcome.⁹⁸ Further research is needed to understand how remote

programs can be utilized to maintain and improve health.

Telemedicine boasts advantage to those who live distant from specialized care, or who lack

time and resources to attend frequent in-person visits. However, equity in implementation of

internet based-services and research can be stymied by gaps in technology access. One in four

adults with low income are smartphone-only internet users, and a 35% gap exists between low

also exist between racial groups, educational levels, rural versus urban homes, and age

groups.⁹⁹ Digital inclusion and equity is considered a super social determinant of health, as it

directly impacts other social determinants of health, from jobs, to education, to medical care.¹⁰⁰

Nevertheless, early studies in telemedicine demonstrate promise not only for ease of patient

interaction, but also for early detection of progressive disease.

Diagnostic complexity, delays and misdiagnosis plague patients with ILD⁶² and sarcoidosis,¹¹

and post-diagnosis are followed by complexity of disease management.⁶ Complex care models

prioritizing comprehensive multidisciplinary approaches in ILD and sarcoidosis are needed.¹⁰¹

Models including multidisciplinary discussion and specialized clinics report notable success, 102

and can be offered remotely to increase external access.¹⁰³ Dedicated ILD clinics not only

improve survival but do so without increasing resource utilization.¹⁰² To improve awareness of

clinics and centers with expertise, patient-advocacy organizations such as the Pulmonary

Fibrosis Foundation (PFF)¹⁰⁴ and the Foundation for Sarcoidosis Research (FSR)¹⁰⁵ have

developed clinic and center of excellence designations; however, access to these specialized

clinics remains a barrier. In the US as of August 2022, 16 states lack a PFF center and 20

states lack an FSR-recognized sarcoidosis clinic. Until additional centers and clinics are

opened, interventions such as Project ECHO (Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes),

can connect providers with specialists through tele-mentoring to improve patient outcomes.¹⁰⁶

Innovation in complex clinical care and collaboration with patient advocacy organizations is

imperative to ensure access to high quality care for all.

health literacy education, which improves outcomes,¹¹¹ is imperative. Designing self-

management programs in ILD and sarcoidosis requires investigation of the complex factors that

influence behaviors around self-management.

Conclusion

The roster of health disparities in ILD and sarcoidosis encompasses socioeconomic,

race/ethnicity, sex/gender, and geographic differences. Flurries of recent publications document

disparities, but we stand at the dawn of understanding and intervening upon the drivers. ILD

and sarcoidosis sorely need energetic funding of cross-institutional and disciplined research,

respiratory health policy making, and multi-faceted patient-centered care. Patient advocacy

groups, researchers, clinicians and policy makers must all have seats at the table and work

collaboratively in a team approach.

The roster of health disparities in ILD and sarcoidosis encompasses socioeconomic, race/ethnicity, sex/gender, and geographic differences.
